explicit_text: My full name is Porter Geneal Peeples Sr., born October 29, 1945, at Notre Dame Hospital in Lynch, Kentucky. I grew up in Lynch, a town built by United States Steel, where my father worked as a coal miner. Fishing with my dad at Tennessee Valley lakes like Douglas Lake and Cherokee Lake was a cherished part of my childhood. I married twice, first to Billye Morrow, with whom I had a son named Porter, and later to Wilma from Hazard, Kentucky.

explicit_text: My father was Roy O'Neal, who came from Bessemer, Alabama, to work in the coal mines in Lynch, Kentucky. He was divorced before leaving Alabama and later married my mother, Carrie Lou Peeples. He was part of a family tradition where brothers like Harden Peeples followed him to Lynch for mining work. Roy O'Neal was a coal miner and a father to nine children, including me.

explicit_text: Bessemer, Alabama, is where my father, Roy O'Neal, originally came from before moving to Lynch, Kentucky, to work in the coal mines. It was a place he left behind in search of better opportunities in mining. The migration from Bessemer to Lynch was part of a larger pattern encouraged by men like Limehouse who brought African American workers northward. My father's roots in Bessemer shaped part of our family's story.

explicit_text: Lynch, Kentucky, is my hometown, built by United States Steel to house coal miners and their families. I was born there in 1945 at Notre Dame Hospital, and I remember the company-built houses, the Big Store, and the community life vividly. Fishing trips to nearby lakes were a big part of growing up in Lynch, and the town had a strong sense of community despite the challenges of segregation. My family lived there for many years, and it shaped much of who I am.

explicit_text: My mother, Carrie Lou Peeples, came from Chattanooga, Tennessee, before marrying my father and moving to Lynch, Kentucky. She was widowed when her first husband was killed in the coal mines in Lynch. Chattanooga was her original home, and her background there was part of the family history I grew up with. Her experiences and resilience influenced our family life in Lynch.

explicit_text: My mother was Carrie Lou Peeples, née Conner, originally from Chattanooga, Tennessee. She was married before and lost her first husband in the coal mines of Lynch. After marrying my father, Roy O'Neal, she raised nine children with him in Lynch. She enjoyed sewing with her Singer sewing machine and was active in church and community life. Her strength and care were central to our family.

explicit_text: United States Steel was the company that built and owned the town of Lynch, Kentucky, where I was born and raised. They constructed the houses miners rented and invested in community facilities like the hospital and stores. The company had a reputation for taking good care of workers, partly due to union support, which attracted many families like mine from Alabama. My father worked for United States Steel as a coal miner, and the company's presence shaped our daily lives.

explicit_text: International Harvester was the company associated with Benham, Kentucky, a neighboring coal town to Lynch. While Lynch was built by United States Steel, Benham was linked to International Harvester, and there were notable differences between the two companies' operations. I heard stories comparing the two, but my direct experience was with United States Steel in Lynch. The distinction between these companies was part of the regional coal mining culture.

explicit_text: The Tennessee Valley Authority created lakes like Douglas Lake and Cherokee Lake, where my father and I often went fishing. These lakes were popular fishing spots for coal miners from eastern Kentucky, including those from Lynch. We would camp on islands and fish at night, enjoying the natural beauty and bonding with family and friends. The TVA lakes provided a recreational escape from the mining life.

explicit_text: Douglas Lake was one of the Tennessee Valley lakes where my dad and I would go fishing on weekends. We kept our boat at home and traveled there often, camping during the day and fishing at night. It was a special place that connected me to my father and to other fishermen from our community. Those fishing trips at Douglas Lake are some of my most treasured memories.

explicit_text: Cherokee Lake was another favorite fishing destination for my father and me, part of the Tennessee Valley Authority lakes. The lake was a place where people of different races came together through fishing, creating bonds beyond everyday life. Cherokee Lake holds a special place in my heart for those family times.

explicit_text: Bill Turner is a dear friend of mine whose father took him fishing, and we often fished together growing up. Bill remains an important part of my memories and friendships from Lynch.

Despite coming from different towns and backgrounds, fishing has been a common thread that bonds us. The Griffiths represent the community spirit that transcends town lines in the coalfields.
